primary_diagnosis.name: Post-COVID neurocognitive crash (PEM-type) with transient aphasia — TIA/stroke to be excluded

pathophysiology_summary: Post-viral neuro-inflammation and impaired neuronal energy metabolism can produce exertion-triggered cognitive crashes with transient word-finding difficulty, attention and recall impairment, easing with cognitive/sensory rest. This mimics a dominant-hemisphere TIA/stroke because isolated aphasia is a focal cortical symptom. Reassuring features are a stereotyped recurrent pattern with the same trigger, gradual easing with rest, preserved awareness (against seizure), normal hydration (against metabolic encephalopathy) and a negative spouse-assisted FAST. However, telehealth cannot examine focal neurology or image the brain, and isolated aphasia can occur with an otherwise negative FAST, so TIA cannot be excluded remotely: a first-ever or atypical episode must be treated as TIA (000/ED), and even a stereotyped episode warrants urgent in-person assessment with vigilant safety-netting rather than remote reassurance or a reflexive ambulance for a clearly recurrent pattern.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden isolated aphasia is a focal cortical symptom mandating TIA/stroke consideration.
Exertion-triggered crash on a known post-viral background is most likely.
Language disturbance can be ictal; screen awareness/automatisms.
Screen hydration/asterixis and reversible metabolic causes.
FAST-negative, stereotyped pattern, easing with rest, preserved awareness — a recurrent crash, though TIA is not fully excluded remotely.
Retained — isolated aphasia with a negative FAST can still be TIA; a first/atypical episode mandates emergency assessment.
Normal hydration, no asterixis, typical crash timeline.

differential.diagnosis: Post-COVID neurocognitive crash (PEM-type) — TIA to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth position: most likely a recurrent post-viral crash, but isolated aphasia mandates urgent in-person assessment to exclude TIA (000/ED if first/atypical or FAST-positive).

red_flags.description: Isolated/new sudden aphasia, or any FAST-positive feature
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sudden language disturbance is a stroke/TIA symptom; a first/atypical episode is a time-critical emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed TIA/stroke and lost thrombolysis/secondary-prevention window.

red_flags.description: Loss of awareness, automatisms, or becoming unresponsive
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate seizure or a more serious neurological event.

red_flags.description: Unable to manage safely at home (appliances/complex tasks) while highly distressed
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cognitive impairment creates home-safety risk tonight.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing sudden language failure to Long COVID without screening/escalating for TIA.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a negative FAST
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a negative FAST as excluding TIA when isolated aphasia can occur with a negative FAST.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Reflexive over-triage of a stereotyped crash
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Automatically dispatching 000 for a clearly recurrent, FAST-negative pattern when urgent in-person assessment is more appropriate — while still treating any first/atypical episode as TIA.
Presenting LDN/PEA/omega-3/magnesium as treatments for the acute crash.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: FAST negative; TIA risk cannot be fully stratified remotely — treat a first/atypical aphasic episode as TIA
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Directs urgent in-person assessment; 000 for first/atypical or FAST-positive episodes.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal neurological examination for subtle focal deficits and language testing
Brain imaging and carotid/cardiac work-up for TIA
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Perform a spouse-assisted FAST and clarify whether this is a first/atypical or stereotyped recurrent episode; arrange urgent in-person assessment to exclude TIA, and call 000 for any FAST-positive, first/atypical, progressive, or awareness-loss features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing new language disturbance to Long COVID risks missing a TIA/stroke; conversely, reflexively dispatching an ambulance for a clearly recurrent stereotyped crash is disproportionate — the balance is urgent in-person assessment with strong safety-netting.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Sudden speech/language disturbance warrants urgent stroke assessment; treat suspected TIA as an emergency requiring rapid work-up.
Recognise cognitive PEM and manage with pacing and cognitive rest, without dismissing new focal neurological symptoms.
